message:
fix(pool): coalesce cache must store completion time, not entry time (#105)

Self-review find. The coalesce wrapper around
`reconstruct_sqlite_file_with_archive_salvage` captured `Instant::now()`
once at function entry and used that same instant both for the lookup
and for the subsequent cache store. But between those two uses sits the
actual reconstruct — which takes O(seconds) on a small mailbox and can
run much longer on larger archives — so the timestamp that lands in the
cache is already stale by the duration of the work it represents.

Concretely: a reconstruct that genuinely takes 15 s would land a 15-s-old
timestamp. The very next sibling caller — the exact thread of callers
the coalesce was built to protect — would then see `age = 15 s + wait`,
miss the 10 s `RECONSTRUCT_COALESCE_WINDOW`, and redo the work. Worse,
slower reconstructs (where duplicate work costs the most) were exactly
the case that coalescing silently failed on.

Fix: call `Instant::now()` fresh right before storing, after the
reconstruct has returned `Ok(stats)`. The lookup still uses the
entry-time instant; they're measuring different things and shouldn't
share a `now`.

Added a unit test —
`recent_reconstruct_cache_store_uses_completion_time_not_entry_time` —
that simulates a slow reconstruct by passing a fake entry time and a
later completion time to the store function. The test first pins the
broken behavior (storing the entry time → lookup misses past the window)
and then pins the correct behavior (storing the completion time →
lookup hits). If someone regresses the wrapper in future, the
integration effect shows up in the unit test surface.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
